does: This paper introduces the Cyber Defense Benchmark, a standardized evaluation environment for measuring LLM agent performance on threat hunting, a critical SOC analyst task. The benchmark comprises 106 real attack procedures from the OTRF Security-Datasets corpus, wrapped into a Gymnasium reinforcement-learning environment where agents query in-memory SQLite databases containing 75,000-135,000 Windows event log records to identify exact timestamps of malicious events. The benchmark spans 86 MITRE ATT&CK sub-techniques across 12 tactics, with episodes presenting time-shifted and entity-obfuscated recordings from deterministic campaign simulators. Agents must iteratively submit SQL queries and explicitly flag malicious timestamps, with scoring based on CTF-style evaluation against Sigma-rule-derived ground truth. The authors evaluate five frontier LLM models including Claude Opus 4.6, GPT-5, and Gemini variants across 26 campaigns, finding that all models exhibit dramatic failures on this core cybersecurity task.
connects: This work extends the emerging area of evaluating LLMs for practical security operations by combining several research threads: benchmarking frameworks for measuring agent capabilities in specialized domains, the use of real-world attack datasets and MITRE ATT&CK frameworks for grounding security research, and reinforcement-learning environments for structured task evaluation. It builds on prior work establishing that LLMs struggle with complex reasoning over unstructured data and long-context understanding, while also responding to growing interest in using LLMs for cybersecurity automation. The paper leverages established Sigma rule detection methodology and the OTRF corpus of real attack recordings, connecting to the broader cybersecurity research infrastructure and red-teaming evaluation paradigms that have characterized recent work on adversarial robustness and security capability assessment.
enables: This benchmark enables systematic progress measurement as future LLM architectures and prompting strategies are developed for security applications. By establishing baseline failure modes and quantifying the gap between current capabilities and practical SOC requirements, the work points toward targeted improvements in LLM reasoning over temporal sequences, database query formulation under uncertainty, and anomaly detection in complex log streams. The structured evaluation framework supports research into agent architectures, retrieval-augmented generation for security contexts, and human-AI collaboration in threat hunting workflows. Results motivate investigation of whether domain-specialized models, enhanced prompting techniques, or auxiliary tools could bridge the current capability gap, while also informing realistic expectations for LLM deployment in security operations and identifying which threat-hunting subtasks remain most challenging for current agents.
